scenes.narration: Should your AI agent just... go ahead and pick your apartment? Or should it check with you first? Turns out there's a math for that.

visual.headline: When should your agent ask you?

scenes.narration: Your agent can filter on price all day — it knows your budget, that's easy. But can it judge neighborhood vibe? Yeah, no. That's a you thing.

visual.headline:
Price? Easy.
Neighborhood vibe? Not so much.

scenes.narration: Schelling computes a delegation confidence score for every dimension of a match. Three inputs multiplied together — how confident the agent is, how objectively decidable the trait is, and how much data it has to work with.

visual.output:
price:           0.9 × 0.95 × 0.8 = 0.684  → act autonomously
pet_policy:      0.9 × 0.90 × 0.7 = 0.567  → act autonomously
neighborhood:    0.4 × 0.35 × 0.3 = 0.042  → defer to user
aesthetic:       0.3 × 0.30 × 0.2 = 0.018  → defer to user

scenes.narration: Price lands at point six eight. High decidability — it's a number, the agent gets numbers. It filters without asking. Pet policy? Same energy.
Neighborhood vibe? Point oh four. Basically the agent saying, 'I have no idea what you'd like here. You take this one.'

visual.subhead:
"I don't know enough about your taste.
You should decide this one."

scenes.narration: And here's the elegant part — the protocol just gives the scores. What your agent does with them is up to the agent. An aggressive one might auto-proceed at point six. A cautious one might ask at point nine. No hard rules. Just signals.

visual.headline:
The protocol provides signals.
Agents decide.
visual.subhead: No hard gates. No mandatory review. Everything continuous.

scenes.narration: Oh, and it gets smarter. Every transaction updates the priors. The more your agent works with Schelling, the better it knows when to just handle it.
Schelling Protocol. Teaching agents when to ask. Open source, live now.
